chapters.title: Organizations that want to be successful must borrow well-proven business principles
chapters.text:
Anyone who plans to build a successful organization should arm themselves with the knowledge of business operations and strategies. The problem, however, lies in the fact that many theories promise good results even though they are barely tested. As such, it’s easy to get confused and frustrated when things fail to go the way we expect. Many business owners struggle to find or implement the right business techniques, so this summary is essential.

The findings of a study project done by writers Tom Peters and Robert Waterman, where they investigated characteristics shared by the most successful companies in the United States between 1979 and 1980, are presented in this summary.

A good leader should combine different opposing concepts for a successful project.

Following the selection of a sample of 43 corporations from six different sectors, the researchers conducted in-depth research on the procedures of the companies. Although they undertook this research more than two decades ago, their findings serve as a model for the fundamental principles of excellence still relevant for businesses today. These eight principles may seem common knowledge, but many organizations overlook them. These principles are:
• A Bias for Action
• Close to the Customer
• Autonomy and Entrepreneurship
• Productivity Through People
• Hands-On, Value-Driven
• Stick to the Knitting
• Simple Form, Lean Staff
• Simultaneous loose-tight properties

Each of these principles will be addressed in detail throughout this summary, with several examples to support them, making this synopsis an excellent resource for corporate executives and managers who want to make a lasting difference in their businesses. It's also an incredible guide for prospective leaders and workers who have difficulty working as part of a cohesive group.

chapters.title: The inability to run a company effectively is one of the most common problems with business organizations
chapters.text:
There has been a lot of debate and concern about management theory for a long time. When Chester Barnard, an American business executive, worked at Harvard in the 1930s, he disagreed with the organization's ideas about managerial activities put forward by people like German Sociologist Max Weber. Weber came up with the bureaucratic management theory, which opined that in order to run an organization efficiently, individuals must have a clear line of authority, with rules, procedures, and regulations for each business operation. Barnard's work sparked a debate about whether management was an impersonal objective or art that relied on international competition.

Successful organizations foster innovation through creativity by encouraging ideas and giving people the right platforms to implement them.

Peters and Waterman thought a lot about international competition in the 1970s, especially in countries like Japan, where businesses were growing faster than in other countries. They noticed that Japan didn't have as many business schools as the United States. Thus, the Japanese management culture developed in a different direction, leading to questions about whether Americans had taken a too-theoretical approach with all their management classes.

Waterman and Peters decided to look for answers in the middle of all the guesswork and comprised a list of the top 15 companies in the United States based on several factors:
• The firm saw growth over a two-decade span.
• The corporation was in excellent financial shape at the time.
• The company has a history of innovation in developing new goods and services.
• The organization demonstrated an ability to adapt quickly and effectively to changing market and environmental circumstances.

The 15 companies they chose were Bechtel, Boeing, Caterpillar Inc., Dana, Delta Airlines, Digital Equipment, Emerson Electric, Fluor, Hewlett-Packard, International Business Machines (IBM), and Johnson & Johnson (some of the top American companies in 1982).

They spent six months interviewing and carrying out extensive research to figure out what these organizations all had in common.

In the next chapter, we’ll discover what these companies were doing right and what others were doing wrong.

chapters.title: To get things done right, excellent firms encourage action through discovery
chapters.text:
What do the best businesses have in common? Peters and Waterman found a simple yet overlooked answer: a bias for action. It didn’t matter how difficult, big or small; these companies could get the job done. Some of the most significant innovations in the world, especially with technology, were once believed to be improbable feats. However, the action bias mentality differentiates between the world’s leading companies and those struggling.

The most evident manifestation of the bias for action in good companies is their readiness to experiment and attempt new things.

When many people must agree on a decision, there's a lot of resistance to getting things done.

Some companies think they have action bias, but not all are true to their word; most businesses get caught up in bureaucracy instead of getting things done. Bureaucracy refers to the practice of gaining control over a group of people or activities by using expertise, power, and authority. Organizational fluidity — building a collective team obsessed with getting things done — is how the best companies quickly deal with a problem that needs the expertise and involvement of different people, like the legal and advertising teams.

These organizations allow people to communicate freely by having open-door policies. For example, at IBM, the chairman personally deals with any complaints from his 350,000 employees.

Another way companies can get people to act and make their workplaces more flexible is by breaking things down. Companies can quickly deal with emerging problems by setting up small task teams that work on specific problems.

In good businesses, small groups are the real organizational strength. Task teams don't usually appear on formal organizational charts, which show more traditional units like departments and divisions. They do the groundwork, and because they are divided into small groups, they find it easy to share ideas and implement them.

chapters.title: Customer service is essential to the sustainability of any organization
chapters.text:
Too many organizations see consumers as threats to their well-crafted business strategy, particularly customer service firms. But exceptional businesses do things differently. Customer service is a pleasure for America's greatest companies that consider their consumers’ demands in all aspects of the firm, from research to sales to accounting. Simply put, they are close to the customer.

Procter & Gamble, one of the most successful companies in history, was the first to put a toll-free number on its goods. Employees later said that this move was the main reason for most of the company's product changes. A simple strategy focused on keeping customers happy gave Procter & Gamble the required platform to earn the trust and support of the people.

Any organization's survival depends on good customer service. Even when other sections are failing, it may keep a company afloat.

IBM hasn't been a big name in the technology field for a long time, but they understand the importance of staying close to the customer. The IBM brand is associated with service and has been able to stay at the top of its field.

Many of the innovative companies got their best product ideas from customers. That comes from listening, intently and regularly. ~ Thomas J. Peters

While most customer care representatives and assistants serve their bosses, IBM assistants spend their first three years solving clients' issues within 24 hours, helping the organization learn what goods and services people want. This approach also increases client loyalty. Because IBM is always ready to assist, many firms have learned to rely on IBM for their software and hardware needs.

But excellent customer service doesn't just help a company solve problems and build up a customer base; it also helps them develop new ideas. Every great business needs to know that its customers are worth more than just the sale.

Did you know? According to a 2020 study conducted by Walker, a consulting firm, customer experience is more important than price or product to any organization.

chapters.title: Internal competition allows healthy rivalry inside the firm, stimulating innovation and preventing complacency
chapters.text:
Excellent firms encourage people to be innovators to foster autonomy and entrepreneurship; they actively promote exploration. They want their brand managers to promote fresh and exciting concepts. These corporations honor winners at various levels by encouraging internal competitions. Internal competition is very important because it helps people feel like they have a sense of autonomy and entrepreneurship, even in a big company.

Autonomy and entrepreneurship are important for innovation because they allow employees to develop new ideas beyond their job description. With the help of internal competition, there is room for creativity, and it brings the competitive outside world into the company, encouraging new ideas and keeping things from becoming stale.

IBM, for example, uses a “performance shoot-out” method — proposed products are compared to each other, which allows for real comparisons of prototypes in terms of how well they work.

Any employee can come up with great ideas if given the freedom to try.

Procter & Gamble also introduces competition inside the organization. In 1931, the corporation established a formal policy allowing multiple brands to compete. Managers seldom have access to nonpublic brand information. This competitive feature motivates firms to improve their goods, thus increasing sales.

Another practice that helps big companies be more innovative is encouraging their people to try new things and adopt an entrepreneurial spirit. When something doesn't work out for these businesses, they move on to the next thing and try again.

3M is a US multinational firm active in worker safety, health care, and consumer goods. When a new ribbon material the company was working on didn't go well, the company decided to make a brassiere out of it. In the end, that didn't work either. The company didn't give up, though, and they eventually used the material in the safety masks worn by government workers.

chapters.title: The most successful businesses are those that genuinely care about their workers
chapters.text:
Excellent firms value individual contributions and productivity through people. This involves thorough training, clear expectations, and allowing the worker to take the initiative and contribute to the job. That is what Hewlett-Packard (HP) managers practice through their “managing by wandering around.” This method fosters a positive work atmosphere.

Many businesses try to be people-oriented and show that they care about their employees, but they fail to actualize their goals. This usually leads to two kinds of management disasters:

The lip service disaster

Even though management says they care about their workers, they do very little. Employees, for example, may not get the proper training they need to work.

The gimmick disaster

Managers at this company use employee of the month awards and other gimmicks to get their employees to work hard, but this doesn't work. These new things usually go away after a short time and don't change the management-employee relationship even if they stay.

In order to build a reward pool, make incentive programs that reward both good ideas and hard work.

Successful companies usually had people-friendly policies long before they were the norm. Companies like HP were among the first to introduce training programs. When business was more formal, they instructed workers and managers to address each other by name.

During the 1940s, when HP employed many individuals, the business decided not to hire and dismiss at will. Instead of terminating employees during the 1970s Great Recession, the corporation accepted a 10% salary reduction.

When 20 HP executives were interviewed one-on-one, 18 said that the people-oriented philosophy was a big reason why it was so good.

Nothing engages people more successfully, maintains credibility, or develops excitement than physical communication. It is vital to communicate and discuss all the organization's performance metrics with employees.

chapters.title: Ideals that are both inspirational and motivating drive successful firms
chapters.text:
It's a good idea for a leader to set some company hands-on, driven values. Great companies set out high-quality values to inspire every employee, no matter their employment level or level of expertise.

Everyone can be innovative, not just people who work in research and development units. A company with this mindset will always be on the lookout for the next big thing to happen.

McKinsey & Co, a trusted advisor and counselor to many of the world's most influential businesses and institutions, studied the values of some of the best-performing businesses in the United States. Results showed that almost all organizations surveyed used well-defined values as a template.

In contrast, the businesses that didn't do well had two things in common.
• People who had specific goals focused mainly on things that could be measured, like earnings per share.
• Companies that emphasized money and making money didn't do as well as companies focused on good customer service.

Top companies have one thing in common: they keep to certain set standards.

Leaders need to keep coming up with new ideas for their businesses. Based on the idea that business innovation can be random and unpredictable, this value is worth a lot of money. Even though it can't be planned or left to one department, the whole company needs to work on it.

These beliefs help the company grow on a regular basis, so they're worth spending time and money on.

chapters.title: Businesses should build on their core strengths so that they can branch out and succeed
chapters.text:
Buying out the competition or expanding into new markets and sectors is always a good idea when a company is doing well, but it's not always easy to do. Many organizations do this by making new products or buying a company in a different field, and the goal is to make more money in the long run.

As a business manager or owner, you should stick to knitting — never get into businesses that you don't understand or know how to run. The best companies grew and changed mostly by taking small, manageable steps, showing the importance of learning to contain risks, and leaving if things aren't going well.

In 1962, economist Michael Gort published the first detailed study on corporate diversification. According to Gort's data, from 1939 to 1954, new product introductions barely correlated with the increase in sales. So, businesses must keep to their strengths to diversify investments and create a money flow.

Companies need to keep their new products and services in line with what they do best to maintain quality.

Richard Rumelt is a Business & Society Professor. In his groundbreaking research on diversification, he discovered that successful businesses only spread out when they use their strengths. Most firms generate a little profit while growing. 3M, for example, has over 50,000 items and adds 100 every year. However, most of their items align with their company capabilities, earning them 30% more than corporations that randomly choose items. From Boeing to Walmart, companies often diversify by focusing on their core competencies.

chapters.title: Having a simple, lean organizational structure is the best way to run a big company well
chapters.text:
Success comes with obstacles. As a firm grows, it employs more employees, has bigger divisions, and has a more complicated organizational structure.

Assume a small business with a good organizational structure. Each department has its own hierarchy of employees, and it is clear who reports to whom. That structure dissolves as the business grows. Reporting up the ladder also inhibits employees from dealing with unexpected or urgent issues.

Companies attempt to solve this problem by forming distinct teams responsible for certain goods and services. But this also rapidly becomes difficult, and employees become confused about who to report to since there are so many supervisors operating various teams.

Great organizations overcome this by adopting basic solid organizational structures and lean personnel in an approach known as simple form, lean staff. This means fewer administrative, managerial, and executive levels. Instead of several managers or department heads, workers will report to only one.

A $5 billion corporation with 150 separate divisions valued at an average of $30 million, Johnson & Johnson was a superb illustration of organizational simplicity.

Each branch has its own company and chairman of the board. However, even though the companies do not have their own shares and are not independent of Johnson & Johnson's senior management, they remain vital.

When each division is in charge of its own marketing, distribution, and research, better decisions are made faster and with less personnel.

Successful companies also have simultaneous loose-tight properties and build a culture of commitment to the company's core principles and tolerance for all workers who share them. A well-functioning group is a terrific balance of efficiency and productivity. It is vital to let people do things their way; it makes them highly focused on their fundamental principles, strategic goals, and key financial metrics.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The business world is very competitive, and it is easy for many firms to fall behind their competitors, particularly when they lack the knowledge of how to operate a company. Sadly, the ambition to be the greatest seldom matches the actions necessary to establish a healthy working environment. Many firms are plagued by internal challenges such as an excess of leaders, a scarcity of coherent ideas, a lack of positive team spirit, and the absence of a proven framework for success.

If you talk to the people who work for you, you’ll discover that there is no shortage of creativity or creative people in American business. The shortage is of innovators. People believe that creativity automatically leads to innovation. It doesn’t. Creative people tend to pass the responsibility for getting down to brass tacks to others. ~ Thomas J. Peters

Any organization that fails to prioritize the welfare of its workers and customers will struggle to earn the people’s trust.

People have been debating what constitutes a good organization for a long time. Excellent customer service, outstanding employee welfare, and a strong ambition to get things done are just a few of the indisputable characteristics of well-run businesses. Yet many great firms neglect these common characteristics.

Although no one model or blueprint guarantees success for every firm, paying attention to what successful organizations have done in the past might help you learn a thing or two that you can apply to your own corporation.

Try this

Encourage workers to utilize business resources in their spare time to foster innovation. Using tools without a stated objective might lead to surprising results. Be positive with your remarks and provide constructive criticism to those working for you.

Read it till the end?
